title: DAVID — Sumerian: The First Written Language

gate_0.music_plan: original score only — music cleared, cue sheet on file

brand.title: DAVID · The Archive
brand.subtitle: Dead languages, actually pronounced.
brand.cta: Bring the dead tongues back — one attestation at a time. DAVID.

beats.speech_text: I am DAVID. Today, Sumerian — not Indo-European, not Semitic — the first language we meet written in clay, five thousand years before our microphones existed.
When writing begins, sound is already slipping away. Sumerian outlived its speakers as a scholarly tongue in Babylon — but the tablets outlasted every throat that knew it natively.
How do you pronounce a language whose last native generation vanished while scribes still copied its signs?
Administrative tablets, royal hymns, and bilingual Sumerian-Akkadian word lists give pronunciations in syllables. Later Mesopotamian lexicons even gloss Sumerian for students who no longer spoke it.
We assemble the corpus: syllabic spellings, Emesal liturgical variants, Akkadian renderings in the same line, and comparative Afro-Asiatic hypotheses — marking every step that is inference.
Syllable values from contemporary scribes are our strongest attestation. Vowel quality in open syllables and pitch remain reconstructed — I separate them before you hear a line.
Listen — dnin-ĝir-su-ka — in the name of Ningirsu — syllabically parsed from a Lagash administrative tablet, with reconstructed vowel length.
Follow the Archive — back to the first hand that pressed a reed into wet clay and called it speech.
